agents.name: old man
agents.description: The old man is a good and poor person who lives with his wife in their village. He goes to the mountains to gather sticks for their fire.

events.name: Introduction to Past Time
events.description: If you’ll believe me there was a time when the fairies were none so shy as they are now. That was the time when beasts talked to men, when there were spells and enchantments and magic every day, when there was great store of hidden treasure to be dug up, and adventures for the asking.At that time, you must know, an old man and an old woman lived alone by themselves. They were good and they were poor and they had no children at all.
thoughts: The text describes a past time period and introduces characters without any conflict or action. It's more of an exposition setting up the story rather than driving it forward.
The text describes a past time when magic and adventure were common, setting the stage for the story. It's an introductory or setup event, not part of the main action.

events.name: Old Couple's Daily Routine
events.description: One fine day, “What are you doing this morning, good man?” says the old woman.“Oh,” says the old man, “I’m off to the mountains with my billhook to gather a faggot of sticks for our fire. And what are you doing, good wife?”“Oh,” says the old woman, “I’m off to the stream to wash clothes. It’s my washing day,” she adds.So the old man went to the mountains and the old woman went to the stream.
thoughts: The event is part of the main narrative action: it introduces the daily routine of the old couple and sets the context for further events. It does not drive the story forward through conflict or external help.

events.name: Woman Finds Lucky Peach
events.description: Now, while she was washing the clothes, what should she see but a fine ripe peach that came floating down the stream? The peach was big enough, and rosy red on both sides.“I’m in luck this morning,” said the dame, and she pulled the peach to shore with a split bamboo stick.By-and-by, when her good man came home from the hills, she set the peach before him. “Eat, good man,” she said; “this is a lucky peach I found in the stream and brought home for you.”
thoughts: The event is a small, everyday occurrence that doesn't drive the main plot forward. It's more of a peaceful moment between the old couple.

The event involves the hero acting within the core plot. The old woman finds a peach in the stream and brings it home for her husband, which is part of their daily routine and contributes to the overall narrative.

The event of finding a peach in the stream is part of the main narrative action and drives the story forward. It's not a conflict-resolution or a climactic moment, but rather an everyday occurrence that sets up further events.

events.name: Peach bursts into baby.
events.description: But the old man never got a taste of the peach. And why did he not?All of a sudden the peach burst in two and there was no stone to it, but a fine boy baby where the stone should have been.“Mercy me!” says the old woman.“Mercy me!” says the old man.The boy baby first ate up one half of the peach and then he ate up the other half. When he had done this he was finer and stronger than ever.
thoughts: The event involves the hero acting, reacting, or being acted upon within the core plot. The old man and his wife are central characters in the story, and their actions drive the narrative forward.

The event involves the old man reacting to a magical occurrence that resolves the initial setup of the story. The peach's transformation and the baby's appearance are unexpected plot developments that drive the narrative forward.
The event is a sudden and unexpected twist that introduces a new element to the story. The peach bursts into two and a baby appears in its place, which is a surprising and magical occurrence.

events.name: Momotaro Born in Peach
events.description: “Momotaro! Momotaro!” cries the old man; “the eldest son of the peach.”“Truth it is indeed,” says the old woman; “he was born in a peach.”Both of them took such good care of Momotaro that soon he was the stoutest and bravest boy of all that country-side. He was a credit to them, you may believe. The neighbours nodded their heads and they said, “Momotaro is the fine young man!”

events.name: Momotaro Requests Food Supplies
events.description: “Mother,” says Momotaro one day to the old woman, “make me a good store of kimi-dango” (which is the way that they call millet dumplings in those parts).“What for do you want kimi-dango?” says his mother.“Why,” says Momotaro, “I’m going on a journey, or as you may say, an adventure, and I shall be needing the kimi-dango on the way.”
thoughts: The event involves Momotaro asking his mother for food before embarking on a journey/adventure. This is a preparatory action that drives the story forward.

events.name: Momotaro Prepares for Journey
events.description: “Where are you going, Momotaro?” says his mother.“I’m off to the Ogres’ Island,” says Momotaro, “to get their treasure, and I should be obliged if you’d let me have the kimi-dango as soon as may be,” he says.So they made him the kimi-dango, and he put them in a wallet, and he tied the wallet to his girdle and off he set.
thoughts: The event involves Momotaro preparing for his journey and asking his mother for kimi-dango, which suggests that it's part of the main narrative action. The story is still in its setup phase, establishing characters and their relationships.

events.name: Momotaro Departs Home
events.description: “Sayonara, and good luck to you, Momotaro!” cried the old man and the old woman.“Sayonara! Sayonara!” cried Momotaro.He hadn’t gone far when he fell in with a monkey.“Kia! Kia!” says the monkey. “Where are you off to, Momotaro?”

events.name: Momotaro Acquires Dumplings
events.description: Says Momotaro, “I’m off to the Ogres’ Island for an adventure.”“What have you got in the wallet hanging at your girdle?”“Now you’re asking me something,” says Momotaro; “sure, I’ve some of the best millet dumplings in all Japan.”

events.name: Momotaro Prepares Battle Plan
events.description: “Now, brothers,” says Momotaro, “listen to my plan. The pheasant must fly over the castle gate and peck the Ogres. The monkey must climb over the castle wall and pinch the Ogres. The dog and I will break the bolts and bars. He will bite the Ogres, and I will fight the Ogres.”

events.name: Momotaro breaks castle gates.
events.description: Momotaro broke the bolts and bars, and the dog leapt into the castle courtyard. “Bow! Wow! Wow!”The brave companions fought till sundown and overcame the Ogres. Those that were left alive they took prisoners and bound with cords—a wicked lot they were.
thoughts: The event described is part of the main narrative action, where Momotaro and his companions are actively working towards their goal of overcoming the Ogres. The event involves conflict and external help, as they break the bolts and bars to gain access to the castle courtyard.

events.name: Momotaro defeats the Ogres.
events.description: “Now, brothers,” says Momotaro, “bring out the Ogres’ treasure.”So they did.The treasure was worth having, indeed. There were magic jewels there, and caps and coats to make you invisible. There was gold and silver, and jade and coral, and amber and tortoise-shell and mother-of-pearl.
thoughts: The event described is the climax of the story where Momotaro and his companions fight against the Ogres. It's a turning point in the narrative as it marks the culmination of their efforts to retrieve the treasure.

events.name: Momotaro Defeats the Ogres.
events.description: “Here’s riches for all,” says Momotaro. “Choose, brothers, and take your fill.”“Kia! Kia!” says the monkey. “Thanks, my Lord Momotaro.”

events.name: Momotaro Prepares for Battle
events.description: “Ken! Ken!” says the pheasant. “Thanks, my Lord Momotaro.”“Bow! Wow! Wow!” says the dog. “Thanks, my dear Lord Momotaro.”
